Huwa Karu Intercultural Reserve

type
organization
location_lat
-8.8
background_section
## Background Along the Brazil–Peru border in Acre, Huni Kuin communities have long stewarded Amazon forest. About [19,000 Huni Kuin](https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc) people live across **12** reserves here, carrying shamanic practice, arts, and deep ecological knowledge—experience that matters for biocultural futures across the western Amazon. Threats include illegal clearing, timber extraction, conventional agriculture, soil loss, and hunting pressure—forces that can displace communities from territory. At the same time, roughly [107,000 ha of unprotected land](https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc) in the area could host regenerative models able to spread more widely in the basin. ### Origins and Network Huwa Karu Intercultural Reserve forms around a dual aim: sustain Huni Kuin culture and spirituality while restoring forest ecosystems. **Mapu Huni Kuin**, spiritual leader and president of the Indigenous Association Yoshibu, is a visible advocate—linking political voice, ceremony, and defence of territory. The mission is intergenerational self-sufficiency through viable enterprises rooted in Huni Kuin lifeways and adequate infrastructure—starting with wild cacao and wild pepper harvest and trade, strengthening identity, and applying forest coexistence knowledge to habitat recovery and cultural sovereignty.
Sources

temp
issues with location
description
HKY Intercultural Reserve is an Indigenous-led nonprofit initiative in Huni Kuin territory, dedicated to restoring Amazonian ecosystems while preserving and celebrating Huni Kuin culture. It aims to create sustainable livelihoods through agroforestry and cultural revitalization.
id
huwa-karu-intercultural-reserve
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy The reserve is assembling a capital plan toward a [$3.75 million, multi-tier allocation](https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc) for restoration, enterprise integration, and the hub. Success is measured partly in replication: a model other Amazon forest communities might adapt—showing that agroforestry rooted in Huni Kuin practice can regenerate ecosystems and livelihoods together, with sovereignty and territory protection moving in parallel.
website
https://www.instagram.com/mapuhunikuin/
bioregional_context
Situated in the Brazil-Peru border region of Acre, this area is part of the western Amazon and home to rich biocultural diversity. It faces threats from deforestation, extractive industries, and cultural erosion, with significant potential for regenerative Indigenous stewardship.
category
Bioregional Organizer

Huwa Karu Intercultural Reserve
